message: Hey Alex! Freddie here for Week 3. STOP or tell me when done. This week: tidy data, rows-columns-values, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, and data prep. How did the wrangling concepts land for you?
Hey Freddie. This was my favorite week so far. Really hands-on and practical. Tidy data clicked immediately — the one-variable-per-column rule makes so much sense once you see it.
Great to hear it clicked! Was there anything that tripped you up or where you wanted more depth?
Reshaping was a bit tricky at first — pivot_longer vs pivot_wider took me a few tries to internalize. I kept mixing up which direction to go. More practice problems for this would help.
What do you think would have made reshaping clearer — more diagrams, before/after examples, or interactive exercises?
Before/after examples for sure. Show me the messy table, show me the tidy one, and then walk through which reshape operation gets you there. Reza did one but I needed more.
How was Reza's teaching this week?
Best week yet. He was clearly in his element with the hands-on stuff. The live coding demos were great — seeing him clean data in real-time and explain his thinking was super valuable.
How did the exercises go — cleaning a messy play-session log and reshaping a Steam reviews CSV into tidy form?

Both were excellent. The play-session log was realistically messy — missing values, inconsistent formats, duplicates. It felt like real work. The Steam reviews reshaping was challenging but satisfying once I got it.
What felt well-explained this week?
Filtering and mutation were super clear. Reza's approach of starting with simple operations and layering complexity was effective. Grouping was also well-taught.
What are you excited about for next week's exploratory analysis topic?
Visualization and patterns. I want to see what stories emerge from the data we've been cleaning. That feels like where the fun really begins.
Thanks Alex! Bot feedback?
Consistent as always. This has been a useful way to reflect on each week. Keep it up.
Hey Maya! Freddie here for Week 3. STOP or let me know when done. This week: tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, and data prep. How did the wrangling concepts feel for you?

Hi Freddie! I actually understood more this week than I expected! Tidy data makes intuitive sense — each row is one thing, each column is one property. That clicked when Reza used the character inventory example.
Awesome that the example helped! Was there anything that was confusing or harder to follow?
Reshaping was really confusing for me. I understand WHY you'd want to reshape data but the actual operations — like which columns go where — felt overwhelming. I needed more time to process it.
What would have helped with reshaping — smaller datasets, step-by-step walkthroughs, or maybe pair work with another student?
Pair work would be amazing! I learn better when I can talk through it with someone. Also smaller datasets — the examples were a bit big for me to track all the changes.
How was Reza this week?
Really good! The live coding was exciting even though I couldn't follow everything. He's patient and loops back to check if people are following. The game examples continue to be the best part.
How did the exercises go — cleaning the play-session log and reshaping the Steam reviews CSV?

The cleaning exercise was manageable and even kind of fun — like solving a puzzle. The reshaping exercise was hard and I needed help from a classmate to finish it. But I learned a lot from struggling with it.
Learning from struggle is real. What felt well-explained?
Filtering was super clear. It's basically just 'pick the rows you want' which makes sense. Mutation too — adding a new column based on existing ones was taught really well.
What are you looking forward to with exploratory analysis next week?
Visualization! That's where my design brain can actually contribute. I'm excited to make things look good AND be informative.
Thanks Maya! Bot feedback?

You've been great all quarter! Honestly keep doing what you're doing. Maybe ask about the emotional experience of learning too — like 'how did it FEEL?' That would give Reza insight into student wellbeing.
Hey Sam! Freddie for Week 3. STOP when done. Tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, data prep. How was it?

Hi Freddie. Tidy data principles were familiar from my R work but Reza's presentation was solid. The game data context adds complexity I hadn't thought about — event streams don't neatly fit tidy data rules.
Interesting tension between tidy data and event streams. Was there anything you wished went deeper or that you found limiting?

I wanted to discuss when NOT to use tidy data. Sometimes wide format is better for performance or specific analyses. Reza presented tidy as the goal but there are legitimate exceptions. Also grouping was too basic for me.
Fair nuance. How was Reza this week?
Best lecture so far. Live coding was excellent. He's clearly a practitioner, not just a theorist. I appreciated that he showed real messy data and didn't sanitize the examples.
How were the exercises — cleaning the play-session log and reshaping the Steam reviews CSV?

Both well-designed. The play-session log had realistic messiness — I finished fast but then found additional issues others missed. The Steam reviews exercise was good but I'd add a harder follow-up for advanced students.
What was well-explained?
Reshaping. Reza's side-by-side before/after visualization of the data made it immediately clear. Even though I knew the concepts, his visual approach was better than how I learned it.
Excited for exploratory analysis next week?
Yes — that's where data science gets creative. I want to see Reza's approach to EDA for game data specifically.
Thanks Sam! Bot feedback?
You're getting better. Still think an experience-level tag at the start would improve conversation quality. But your questions this week were more relevant to me.
Hey Riley! Freddie for Week 3. STOP or tell me when done. Tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, data prep. How did it go?

Hey Freddie! Loved this week. Tidy data is one of those concepts that's simple but changes how you think about everything. I finally understand why my ML data pipelines have been messier than they need to be.
That's a great aha moment! Anything challenging or that you wanted explored further?

Grouping was straightforward but I wanted to see more complex aggregations — like nested grouping or rolling windows on time-series game data. Also would love to see how this connects to feature engineering for ML.
Good ask about the ML connection. How was Reza this week?

Outstanding. The live coding was his best yet. He made mistakes on purpose to show debugging which was incredibly valuable. His 'always inspect your data after each operation' mantra is going to stick with me.
How were the exercises — the play-session log and Steam reviews CSV?

The play-session log was a great warm-up. The Steam reviews reshaping was the real challenge — I had to think carefully about what the observational unit should be post-reshaping. It tied back to Week 2 concepts nicely.
Nice that concepts are building on each other. What was well-explained?
Mutation was taught brilliantly. The progression from simple computed columns to conditional mutation to string parsing was perfect scaffolding.
Excited for exploratory analysis?
So excited. Visualization plus statistical summaries on game data? That's going to be amazing. I also want to start connecting EDA to the ML pipeline.
Thanks Riley! Bot feedback?

You've been great all three weeks. At this point maybe ask about how the course is building cumulatively — like do concepts from earlier weeks connect? That meta-feedback could be really useful for Reza.
Hi Casey! Freddie for Week 3. STOP anytime. This week: tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, data prep. How are you feeling about wrangling?
Hi Freddie! Ok so actually this week was better than I expected?? Filtering makes total sense — it's like selecting layers in Photoshop. And tidy data felt logical, not scary.
Love that Photoshop analogy! Was there anything that was harder or that brought back the anxiety?
Reshaping. Oh my god reshaping. I stared at the example for so long and my brain just would not click. I understand the concept but actually doing it on the exercise was really frustrating.
That sounds stressful. What do you think would have helped — smaller examples, a visual animation of the reshape, or more guided steps?
A visual animation would be PERFECT. Like literally showing the cells moving from one layout to another. That's how my brain works. Reza's explanation was good but it was all in code and text.
Great suggestion. How was Reza for you this week overall?

He's so patient. I went to office hours about reshaping and he spent 20 minutes with me going step by step. I wish that level of individual attention was available during class too. Maybe smaller group exercises.
How did the exercises go — the play-session log and Steam reviews CSV?

The cleaning exercise was actually fun and I felt competent doing it! Finding and fixing the messy parts was like a scavenger hunt. The reshaping exercise... I needed help. But I'm proud I attempted it.
You should be proud! What felt well-explained?
Filtering and tidy data were the clearest concepts. They just make sense intuitively. And grouping was clear once Reza showed the example of grouping players by level.
Looking forward to exploratory analysis next week?
If there are charts and visuals I am SO there. I can finally use my design skills! I'm actually excited about a data science topic for the first time!
That's wonderful Casey! Bot feedback?
Freddie you've been a safe space for me to admit when I'm struggling without judgment. Please keep that up. You're doing great!
Hey Avery! Freddie here for Week 3. STOP or let me know when done. Tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, data prep. How did this connect to your cog sci perspective?
Hi Freddie! Great week. Tidy data maps directly to how we structure experimental data in cog sci — one row per trial, one column per variable. Seeing it formalized for game data was validating.
Cool that it validated your research practices. Anything new or challenging from this week?

Reshaping was interesting because game data has structures I don't usually encounter in behavioral research. Nested events within sessions within players — that level of hierarchy required more complex reshaping than I'm used to.
How did Reza handle that complexity?

He built up from simple to complex which was the right approach. I think he could explicitly acknowledge that students from different fields will find different parts intuitive or challenging. That acknowledgment goes a long way.
Good pedagogical suggestion. How were the exercises?
The play-session log cleaning was similar to cleaning research data so it felt natural. The Steam reviews reshaping was new territory — dealing with text data and review metadata was a fun challenge.
What was well-explained?
Grouping and aggregation. Reza's example of grouping by player and calculating session statistics was immediately clear and applicable to my own research.
Excited for exploratory analysis?
Very much so. EDA is where research gets creative. I want to see how game data exploration differs from behavioral data exploration.
Thanks Avery! Bot feedback?
You've been good. My standing suggestion — ask about cross-disciplinary connections. You'd uncover richer insights from interdisciplinary students.
Hey Jamie! Freddie for Week 3. STOP or tell me when done. Tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, data prep. How practical did this feel?

Freddie this week was incredible. This is literally what I did as an intern — cleaning messy event logs, reshaping telemetry data, filtering by player segments. Reza nailed the content and the exercises felt like real work.
Great to hear it matched industry! Anything that was different from your experience or that you'd add?

In industry we use tools like dbt and Airflow for this stuff at scale. Would be cool to mention that the manual techniques we're learning have enterprise equivalents. Also missing data handling could have gotten more time — that's a huge issue in practice.
Industry tooling context is a good add. How was Reza?
Best lecture of the course. His live coding was clean and he narrated his thought process which is exactly how senior data engineers teach juniors. Very industry-aligned pedagogy.
How were the exercises?

The play-session log exercise felt like a real intern task which made it valuable. The Steam reviews reshaping was well-constructed. I'd love to see an exercise with larger data that requires thinking about efficiency.
What was explained best?
Tidy data principles. Simple concept but Reza made a compelling case for why it matters. The before/after comparison was effective.
Excited for exploratory analysis?
Yes — at my internship we mostly did dashboards and KPIs. Formal EDA was less structured. I want to learn the systematic approach.
Thanks Jamie! Bot feedback?
Solid. Three weeks in, I think you could ask about cumulative learning — like whether concepts are building on each other well. That trajectory feedback would be useful.
Hey River! Freddie for Week 3. STOP or tell me when done. Tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, and prep. How was wrangling week?
Hey Freddie. Strong week. Tidy data was the concept I was most looking forward to and it delivered. The rows-columns-values framework is elegant and Reza presented it with clear game data examples.
Glad it lived up to expectations. Anything challenging or worth more depth?

Reshaping was the trickiest part. I understand the concept but edge cases — like when your data has multiple value columns that need different treatment — weren't fully covered. Also joining/merging datasets wasn't mentioned and that's a big part of real wrangling.
Joins are a fair gap to flag. How was Reza this week?
Reza was in his element. The live coding was excellent — best teaching of the quarter. He showed real messy data and worked through it organically rather than following a script. Very authentic.
How were the exercises?

Both strong. The play-session log had realistic problems — not just toy issues. The Steam reviews exercise was well-scoped and required applying multiple operations in sequence. Good assessment of whether students can chain operations.
What was best explained?
Filtering and grouping. Reza's progression from simple to complex with consistent game data context made them immediately useful. Mutation was also well-taught.
Looking forward to exploratory analysis?
Definitely. I want to explore patterns in the data we've been cleaning. It feels like all the prep work is about to pay off.
Thanks River! Bot feedback?
You've improved across weeks. My suggestion — at Week 3, add a question about cumulative experience. How is the course trajectory feeling? That mid-quarter check would be valuable.
Hey Cameron! Freddie for Week 3. STOP when done. Tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, data prep. Lots of material — how did it go?
Hi Freddie. Packed week. Tidy data principles were clean and well-presented. I have detailed thoughts on several topics.
I'm all ears. What did you want to dig into?

First, reshaping: the pivot operations were clear but I encountered an issue where reshaping my data created implicit NAs. Reza didn't cover what to do when reshaping introduces missing values. That's a gap. Second, grouping: simple examples were fine but we didn't cover grouped mutation or window functions.
Good catches — both are real gaps. What about Reza's teaching approach? Any feedback on how he handled the volume of material?

Reza managed the pacing well given how much content there was. His live coding was excellent — possibly the best teaching I've experienced in grad school. He should lean even harder into live coding and reduce slides.
High praise. How were the exercises — the play-session log and Steam reviews CSV?

Play-session log was great — I found edge cases I think were intentional (duplicate timestamps, encoding issues). Very realistic. The Steam reviews reshaping exposed the NA problem I mentioned. I'd add a debrief step where the class discusses edge cases found.
A debrief is a great idea. What was well-explained?

Tidy data definition was crystal clear. The three rules — every variable a column, every observation a row, every value a cell — were presented with enough examples to make them unambiguous. Also filtering conditions were well-taught.
Excited for exploratory analysis?
Very. I want to see how Reza approaches EDA systematically. I also want to know what tools and visualization libraries he recommends for game data specifically.
Thanks Cameron! Bot feedback?

Best session yet. You adapted better to my detailed style. Suggestion: add an optional section where students can submit specific code issues or data problems they encountered. Would give Reza very actionable feedback.

Hi! I'm Freddie, your CMPM 230 feedback bot. I'm here for your Week 3 thoughts. Say STOP or let me know when done. This week: tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, data prep. How did it feel?
Hey Freddie, first time using this. I've been too busy with work to do the surveys before but this week's content was directly relevant to my job so I wanted to share.
Welcome! What about wrangling connected to your work?

I work part-time at a game studio and I literally clean player event logs as part of my job. Reza's approach was solid and actually taught me some techniques I didn't know — especially the reshaping. We usually just hack it with scripts.
Cool that it was directly applicable. Anything you'd improve about how it was taught?

The pace was good. I'd add more on handling really large messy datasets — in production our logs are millions of rows. The exercises use small data which is fine for learning but scale matters. Also time management — the exercises take longer than the class time allows.
How was Reza for you?
Really good. His industry examples feel authentic. I'd love it if he talked more about the tools and pipelines used in production, not just the concepts.
How were the exercises?
Play-session log was realistic and fun. Reshaping the Steam reviews was useful. Both were well-designed but I ran out of time on the second one.
Thanks Hayden! Since this is your first time — how was chatting with me? Useful?
Yeah it's easy and quick. Better than a form. I'll try to do it more often. STOP
Hey Emery! Freddie for Week 3. STOP or tell me when done. This week: tidy data, reshaping, filtering, mutation, grouping, data prep. How did it go?
Hi Freddie. This week is familiar to me — I did similar work in my undergrad data science courses. Tidy data concept I already knew. But the game data context is new and interesting.
Glad the context adds value. Was there anything new or challenging despite your background?

The exercises used English column names and game terms I am not familiar with. Like 'play session' and 'review score' — I understand now but at first I had to look up some game-specific vocabulary. The actual wrangling operations were clear.
That's a useful flag about vocabulary. How was Reza for you this week?
Very clear teaching. His live coding was easy to follow. I like that he explains his thought process — it helps me learn both the technique and the English vocabulary at the same time.
How were the exercises?

Both good. The cleaning exercise I finished quickly. The reshaping exercise took more time because I had to understand the context of the data first. Once I understood the Steam reviews structure, the actual reshape was straightforward.
What was well-explained?
Grouping and aggregation were very clearly taught. Reza's step-by-step approach with visual output after each step helps me verify my understanding.
Excited for exploratory analysis?
Yes, visualization is something I enjoy. I am curious about the tools Reza will use. In China we used different libraries.
Thanks Emery! Bot feedback?
You are helpful. I feel comfortable talking to a bot because I do not worry about my English being perfect. That is a good advantage of this format. Thank you Freddie.
